figures.gloss: The legendary author of the lost Bārhaspatya-sūtras, the materialist charter. Hostile legend makes him the guru of the gods themselves, teaching materialism to the demons to lead them to ruin — the school's founder cast as a divine saboteur.
figures.facet: A founder who exists only in enemy custody: his sutras survive as quotations inside the refutations written to destroy them, and even his origin story was authored by the opposition — the mirror's purest case of a tradition wearing a mask its rivals carved.
figures.provenance: Sūtra fragments quoted in Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha ch. 1 and other doxographies; the demon-delusion frame in Maitrāyaṇīya Upaniṣad 7.9.

figures.gloss: The earliest historically attested Indian materialist — a contemporary of the Buddha who taught that a person is the four elements and nothing more: at death earth returns to earth, water to water, and 'fools and wise alike are cut off and perish; there is nothing after death.'
figures.facet: The caricature confounded in advance: the arch-materialist wore a garment of woven hair, the meanest and most austere of coverings — the annihilationist as ascetic, against every 'eat, drink' slander later stitched onto the school.
figures.provenance: Sāmaññaphala Sutta (DN 2), among the six heterodox teachers.

figures.gloss: The epistemological razor: perception alone is a valid means of knowledge (pramāṇa); inference and scriptural testimony compound error. What cannot be perceived — heaven, soul, karma, the fruits of sacrifice — is thereby dismissed.
figures.facet: Demolition by standard of evidence rather than counter-myth: the whole Vedic edifice is brought down not by rival gods but by tightening the rules for what may count as known — the mirror's oldest surviving evidentialism.
figures.provenance: Cārvāka chapter of Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha (Cowell & Gough trans.); Debiprasad Chattopadhyaya, Lokāyata (1959).

figures.gloss: The self IS the living body: consciousness arises from the four elements rightly combined 'as the intoxicating power arises from fermented grain,' and at death it simply disperses with them. There is no soul to travel and nowhere for it to go.
figures.facet: The kinva (ferment) analogy is a genuine emergentism — mind as a property of configured matter — argued from within a doxography compiled to refute it; the position survives because its enemies quoted it accurately enough to attack.
figures.provenance: Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha ch. 1, including the fermentation simile.

figures.gloss: The surviving Cārvāka verses are jokes with edges: if the śrāddha offering feeds the dead, why provision a traveler — feed him by offering at home; if the beast slain in sacrifice goes to heaven, why does the sacrificer not offer his own father? The Vedas' authors are called buffoons, knaves, and demons.
figures.facet: Satire as the school's surviving genre: the laughter outlived the doctrine because opponents had to quote the jokes in order to refute them — the enemy archive preserving precisely what it meant to bury.
figures.provenance: Verses quoted in the Cārvāka chapter of Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha (Cowell & Gough trans.).

figures.gloss: 8th-century philosopher whose sole surviving work, the Tattvopaplavasiṃha, systematically demolishes every recognized means of knowledge (pramāṇa) — including perception itself — pushing the Cārvāka critique of inference and scripture into a totalizing epistemological skepticism. His precise affiliation is debated: claimed by some scholars as a radical materialist, by others as a Pyrrhonist-style skeptic distinct from mainstream Lokāyata.
figures.facet: He out-radicalizes his own tradition: where standard Cārvāka doctrine trusts perception as the one valid pramāṇa, Jayarāśi denies even that, leaving worldly practice standing on no epistemic warrant at all rather than on materialist certainty.
figures.provenance: Jayarāśi Bhaṭṭa, Tattvopaplavasiṃha (manuscript rediscovered and published by Sukhlalji Sanghavi and Rasiklal C. Parikh, 1940); Eli Franco, Perception, Knowledge and Disbelief: A Study of Jayarāśi's Scepticism (1987, rev. 1994).

figures.gloss: Jayarāśi's sole surviving treatise, arguing that no pramāṇa — perception, inference, testimony, or any other — can be logically validated, so the metaphysical categories built by every rival school (Nyāya, Buddhist, Mīmāṃsā, and Cārvāka's own materialism alike) collapse; worldly life continues regardless, without need of philosophical warrant.
figures.facet: The one complete manuscript to survive from the entire Lokāyata orbit — a school elsewhere 'known only by quotation' turns out, in this single case, to be knowable by its own book, rediscovered only in the 20th century.
figures.provenance: Manuscript rediscovered and first published by Sukhlalji Sanghavi and Rasiklal C. Parikh (1940); English-language study in Eli Franco (1987/1994).

figures.gloss: A chieftain (rājanya) portrayed running physical experiments to test for a soul separable from the body — weighing men before and after death, sealing condemned criminals alive in jars to listen for an escaping spirit — and reporting he found nothing surviving death; preserved as a debate figure in both the Buddhist Pāyāsi Sutta (Dīgha Nikāya 23) and the Jain Paesi-kahāṇayaṃ (Rāyapaseṇaiya).
figures.facet: The most vivid empirical-annihilationist portrait in early Indian literature, kept alive precisely because two rival traditions each needed a formidable materialist opponent to argue down — experimentalist skepticism about the soul predating the Cārvāka school's own systematization.
figures.provenance: Pāyāsi Sutta, Dīgha Nikāya 23 (Pali Canon); Paesi-kahāṇayaṃ / Rāyapaseṇaiya (Jain Śvetāmbara Āgama).

figures.gloss: A c. 7th-century Cārvāka teacher known only from a hostile quotation preserved in Kamalaśīla's Tattvasaṃgraha-pañjikā; credited with conceding that inference is valid for worldly, perceptible matters while still barring its extension to transcendent claims (the soul, the afterlife, unseen causes) — the school's one attested internal refinement rather than a mere founding slogan.
figures.facet: Evidence the Cārvāka position was philosophically alive and adjusting, not a fixed caricature: a named teacher narrowing his own school's epistemology in response to standard objections, preserved only because an opponent thought the concession worth refuting.
figures.provenance: Quoted in Kamalaśīla's Tattvasaṃgraha-pañjikā (commentary on Śāntarakṣita's Tattvasaṃgraha, 8th c.); discussed in Debiprasad Chattopadhyaya, Lokāyata (1959).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: the epistemology and its verdict — admit perception alone as proof, and the unperceived soul dissolves into the perfectly perceivable body.
Intra-tradition bond: the attributed founder and the school's surviving voice — the anti-ritual verses circulate under Brihaspati's name in the doxographies.

GOLD SEAM: both dissolve the afterlife by physics — consciousness ends when the body's elements disperse, and the terror of post-mortem judgment dies with it; a typological kinship, no contact attested.

GOLD SEAM: two independent ancient materialisms grounding knowledge in the senses and reality in matter, each demoting the gods to irrelevance rather than waging war on them — parallel inventions, no transmission attested.
GOLD SEAM (legacy): modern Indian rationalism explicitly reclaims Cārvāka/Lokāyata as its ancestor — Chattopadhyaya's Lokāyata (1959) is the landmark — a documented adoption, not an unbroken descent.
GOLD SEAM: the same conclusion twenty-four centuries apart — death as complete cessation, and this one life made precious precisely because it is the only one.

GOLD SEAM: fellow heterodox teachers among the six of the Sāmaññaphala Sutta — the same Gangetic śramaṇa generation, attested side by side, though doctrinal opposites: annihilation at death against fated aeons of rebirth.
Fellow members of the six heterodox teachers named together in the Sāmaññaphala Sutta (DN 2) — the same Gangetic śramaṇa generation, attested side by side.
Jayarāśi is the treatise's author; the school's one complete authored-and-surviving pair.
Jayarāśi's total skepticism inverts standard Cārvāka epistemology: where Pratyakṣa trusts perception as sole proof, Jayarāśi denies even perception can be validated.
Pāyāsi's jar and scale experiments dramatize empirically the same body-is-the-self claim the Dehātmavāda doctrine argues philosophically.
Purandara's conceded limitation of inference to worldly matters is an internal refinement of the same perception-first epistemology.
fellow figures of the same Gangetic śramaṇa debate-generation, attested in the same canonical dialogues, though doctrinal opposites — Pāyāsi's empirical annihilationism against Ājīvika fatalism.

both ground 'nothing survives death' in something like an empirical method — Pāyāsi's jars and scales, Epicurus's atomist physiology — rather than scriptural authority; independent inventions, no transmission attested.

dossier.essence: The materialist school of ancient India, also called Lokāyata, attributed to the legendary sage Brihaspati and attested in person from Ajita Kesakambali, a contemporary of the Buddha who taught that a human being is the four elements and nothing more. It denied the soul, the afterlife, karma, and the authority of the Vedas, admitting perception alone as a sure means of knowledge. Extinct as a school by the late medieval period, it is preserved almost entirely in the quotations of the opponents who wrote to refute it.
dossier.cosmos: Only the perceivable world of earth, water, fire, and air exists; consciousness arises when the elements combine in the living body 'as the intoxicating power arises from fermented grain,' and at death it disperses with them. There is no heaven, hell, rebirth, or governing deity, and no unseen fruit of sacrifice — what cannot be perceived is thereby dismissed, since inference and scriptural testimony compound error.
dossier.practice: No Cārvāka ritual life, clergy, or institution is attested; the school survives as a philosophical position and a satirical voice — verses ridiculing the śrāddha offering to the dead and the sacrificial victim's promised heaven, calling the Vedas' authors buffoons and knaves. Its opponents caricatured its ethic as gross hedonism ('while life is yours, live joyously'), though the earliest attested materialist, Ajita of the hair-blanket, was an ascetic; modern Indian rationalism has explicitly reclaimed the school as an ancestor.
dossier.canon: The school's charter, the Bārhaspatya-sūtras, is lost and known only by quotation; the principal witnesses are the Cārvāka chapter of Mādhava's Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha (c. 1340) and Ajita Kesakambali's doctrine in the Buddhist Sāmaññaphala Sutta — all opponents' records.
dossier.reflects: Organized doubt inside the religious field — the evidentialist impulse that disciplines what may count as known, preserved solely in the archives of its refuters.
sources: E. B. Cowell and A. E. Gough (trans.), 'The Sarva-darśana-saṃgraha of Mādhava' (1882)
Debiprasad Chattopadhyaya, 'Lokāyata: A Study in Ancient Indian Materialism' (1959)

links.cite: E. B. Cowell and A. E. Gough (trans.), 'The Sarva-darśana-saṃgraha of Mādhava' (1882)
Debiprasad Chattopadhyaya, 'Lokāyata: A Study in Ancient Indian Materialism' (1959)
